target.name: aspterric acid
target.motivation: Aspterric acid has potent herbicide properties and inhibits Arbidopsis pollen development. Previous syntheses were long and low yielding. This work aims for a new, more efficient synthesis.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Robinson annulation and PCl5-mediated ring contraction

success_criteria.free_text: New synthesis of aspterric acid (2) and penigrisacid A (4), reassignment of penigrisacid A structure, and formal synthesis of schisanwilsonene A (3).

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the hydroazulene core
key_disconnections.reasoning: This reaction was previously developed by the group and efficiently forms the bicyclic core from readily available starting materials.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond forming the ether ring in aspterric acid
key_disconnections.reasoning: To close ring C in aspterric acid, this radical cyclization is envisioned.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond forming the epoxide in penigrisacid A
key_disconnections.reasoning: To form the five-membered ring and alpha-hydroxylate the methyl ester simultaneously.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy relies on a common building block (12) derived from a gold(I)-catalyzed cycloisomerization/formal (4+3) cycloaddition. This intermediate is then divergently functionalized to access aspterric acid and penigrisacid A. For aspterric acid, key steps include an oxidation/reduction sequence to invert a stereocenter, a Suárez radical cyclization to form the ether ring, and alpha-hydroxylation. For penigrisacid A, a vanadium-catalyzed tandem epoxidation/SN2' cyclization is used to form the five-membered ring and introduce the alpha-hydroxy ester functionality.

thought.background: The gold(I)-catalyzed cycloisomerization/formal (4+3) cycloaddition is a known reaction for forming the daucane core.
thought.gap: Optimizing conditions to favor intermolecular reaction over unproductive self-cyclization and to obtain the desired silylenol ether intermediate.
thought.decision: To form the hydroazulene core, chose a gold(I)-catalyzed reaction between 1,3-diene 14 and enyne 15, expecting to reduce self-cyclization by slow addition of enyne 15 to a cold solution of 14 and catalyst.

outcome.observation: The cycloaddition was performed, yielding silylenol ether 13. Yield for this step is not explicitly stated but is part of a 3-step sequence yielding 36% for 18.

evidence.text_span: The total synthesis of the common building block 12 commenced with the construction of the hydroazulene core by the gold(I)-catalyzed reaction between 1,3-diene 14 and enyne 15, which were synthesized from commercially available materials in two steps14 and one step,15 respectively (Scheme 2). To favor intermolecular reaction between enyne and diene over unproductive self-cyclization of compound 15, we performed a slow addition of a solution of compound 15 into a cold solution of compound 14 and cationic JohnPhosAu(MeCN)SbF6 in CH2Cl2 . Under these conditions, we could reduce the amount of diene to 1.1 equiv and the gold(I) catalyst loading to 0.5 mol % .
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Silylenol ethers can be oxidized to alpha-hydroxy ketones via Rubottom-type oxidation.
thought.gap: Preventing acid-catalyzed isomerization of the alkene to a conjugated diene during the oxidation.
thought.decision: To introduce the alpha-hydroxy functionality, chose Rubottom-type oxidation with mCPBA, expecting to prevent unwanted isomerization by adding NaHCO3 to quench residual acid.

outcome.observation: Epoxidation of silyl-enol ether and ring opening with MeOH yielded the corresponding acetal intermediate 17. Yield for this step is not explicitly stated but is part of a 3-step sequence yielding 36% for 18.

evidence.text_span: Following the cycloaddition, a Rubottom-type oxidation16 was performed with mCPBA. To prevent the unwanted acidcatalyzed isomerization of alkene to conjugated diene in silyl enol ether 13, NaHCO3 was added to quench any residual acid.17 Epoxidation of silyl-enol ether and ring opening with MeOH yielded the corresponding acetal intermediate 17
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Acetals can be hydrolyzed under acidic conditions to yield ketones.
thought.gap: Ensuring complete hydrolysis and isolation of the desired alpha-hydroxy ketone.
thought.decision: To obtain the alpha-hydroxy ketone, chose acid hydrolysis of acetal 17, expecting to yield 18 as a single diastereomer.

outcome.observation: Acid hydrolysis led to alpha-hydroxy ketone 18 as a single diastereomer in 36% yield over three steps. NOESY and X-ray confirmed relative configuration.

evidence.text_span: which, after acid hydrolysis, led to α-hydroxy ketone 18 as a single diastereomer in 36% yield over three steps. Nuclear Overhauser effect spectroscopy (NOESY) experiments as well as the characterization by X-ray diffraction of a mCBA adduct of the product18 confirmed the relative configuration of product 18.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Secondary alcohols can be protected as TBS ethers.
thought.gap: Ensuring selective protection of the secondary alcohol in the presence of the ketone.
thought.decision: To protect the secondary alcohol, chose TBSCl with imidazole, expecting to obtain 19 in high yield.

outcome.observation: Protection of the secondary alcohol in product 18 with TBSCl gave 19 in 93% yield.

evidence.text_span: Protection of the secondary alcohol in product 18 with TBSCl, followed by triflation using LiHMDS and Comins’ reagent, gave triflate 20 in 93 and 73% yields, respectively.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Ketones can be converted to vinyl triflates, which are useful for further functionalization.
thought.gap: Ensuring efficient triflation of the ketone.
thought.decision: To enable further functionalization, chose triflation of ketone 19 using LiHMDS and Comins’ reagent, expecting to obtain triflate 20 in good yield.

outcome.observation: Triflation using LiHMDS and Comins’ reagent gave triflate 20 in 73% yield.

evidence.text_span: Protection of the secondary alcohol in product 18 with TBSCl, followed by triflation using LiHMDS and Comins’ reagent, gave triflate 20 in 93 and 73% yields, respectively.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Vinyl triflates can undergo palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation.
thought.gap: Achieving efficient carbonylation to introduce the ester group.
thought.decision: To introduce the ester group, chose palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation of triflate 20, expecting to obtain 21 in high yield.

outcome.observation: Palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation of compound 20 gave 21 in 97% yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, the palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation of compound 20 and subsequent tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) removal afforded common intermediate 12 in an excellent yield.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: TBS protecting groups can be removed under acidic conditions.
thought.gap: Ensuring complete deprotection to yield the common intermediate 12.
thought.decision: To deprotect the alcohol, chose TBS removal using (±)-CSA, expecting to afford common intermediate 12 in excellent yield.

outcome.observation: TBS removal afforded common intermediate 12 in 96% yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, the palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation of compound 20 and subsequent tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) removal afforded common intermediate 12 in an excellent yield.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Hydrogenation is typically used to reduce double bonds.
thought.gap: Achieving complete hydrogenation of the diene in 12.
thought.decision: To hydrogenate the diene, chose catalytic Pd/C, expecting to obtain a fully saturated product.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation of diene 12 with catalytic Pd/C resulted in partially reduced intermediates that could not be further hydrogenated.

evidence.text_span: Hydrogenation of diene 12 with catalytic Pd/C resulted in partially reduced intermediates that could not be further hydrogenated.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Pd/C failed to fully hydrogenate the diene.
thought.gap: Finding a catalyst and conditions that achieve complete hydrogenation.
thought.decision: To achieve full hydrogenation, chose Adams’ catalyst (PtO2) with H2 in EtOAc with traces of HOAc, expecting to obtain fully hydrogenated product 22.

outcome.observation: Performing the reaction with 20 mol % Adams’ catalyst in EtOAc with traces of HOAc gave fully hydrogenated product 22 in 82% yield.

evidence.text_span: Performing the reaction with 20 mol % Adams’ catalyst19 in EtOAc with traces of HOAc gave fully hydrogenated product 22 in 82% yield.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Mitsunobu conditions are commonly used for stereochemical inversion of alcohols.
thought.gap: Achieving inversion of the alcohol at C2 in 22.
thought.decision: To invert the configuration of the alcohol at C2, chose Mitsunobu conditions, expecting to obtain the desired epimer.

outcome.observation: Several attempts using Mitsunobu conditions were unsuccessful.

evidence.text_span: To close ring C in aspterric acid, the configuration of the alcohol in C2 had to be inverted. After several attempts using Mitsunobu conditions, we decided to switch to an oxidation/reduction sequence with Dess−Martin periodinane (DMP) and NaBH4/MeOH
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Mitsunobu conditions failed to invert the alcohol at C2.
thought.gap: Finding an alternative method for stereochemical inversion.
thought.decision: To invert the configuration of the alcohol at C2, chose an oxidation/reduction sequence with Dess−Martin periodinane (DMP) and NaBH4/MeOH, expecting to obtain compound 25.

outcome.observation: Oxidation with DMP and reduction with NaBH4/MeOH gave compound 25 in 70% yield over two steps, along with compound 24 (1:1 mixture with 22), which could be recycled.

evidence.text_span: After several attempts using Mitsunobu conditions, we decided to switch to an oxidation/reduction sequence with Dess−Martin periodinane (DMP) and NaBH4/MeOH , giving rise to compound 25 in 70% yield over two steps, together with compound 24 (1:1 mixture with compound 22), which could be recycled back with DMP to compound 23.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Suárez radical cyclization can be used to form ether rings.
thought.gap: Controlling the reaction to minimize byproduct formation and achieve efficient ring closure.
thought.decision: To close the ether ring, chose a Suárez radical cyclization using PhI(OAc)2 and I2, followed by AgNO3, expecting to obtain 16 and minimize lactone byproduct 26.

outcome.observation: The ether ring was closed quantitatively with AgNO3 in DMF after C-H activation with PhI(OAc)2 and I2. The reaction was stopped at ca. 50% conversion to minimize lactone byproduct 26. X-ray diffraction confirmed the relative configuration of stereocenters and ring C formation.

evidence.text_span: The ring closing was then performed via a Suarez ́ radical cyclization.20 First a C-H activation of the methyl group was achieved with PhI(OAc)2 (1.5 equiv) and I2 (1 equiv) in deoxygenated cyclohexane and using a desk lamp (35 W). The formation of lactone byproduct 26 was observed when the reaction time was extended, presumably by activation of the other two methyl C-H bonds, leading to an iodoform intermediate, which undergoes ring closure and hydrolysis of the resulting cyclic diiodomethylene in the aqueous work up. To minimize the formation of compound 26, the reaction was stopped at ca. 50% conversion of compound 25 [monitored by ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC)]. Then, the ether ring was closed quantitatively with AgNO3 in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The structures of compounds 23, 25, and 16 were determined by X-ray diffraction, confirming the relative configuration of the stereocenters at C2 and C6 and the formation of ring C from the desired face of the molecule.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Alpha-hydroxylation of esters can be achieved via enolate formation and reaction with oxygen, followed by reduction.
thought.gap: Achieving selective alpha-hydroxylation of the ester in 16.
thought.decision: To alpha-hydroxylate the ester, chose trapping the potassium enolate with oxygen and reducing the resulting hydroperoxide with triethylphosphite, expecting to obtain compound 27 as a single diastereomer.

outcome.observation: Alpha-hydroxylation of the ester in compound 16 gave compound 27 in 90% yield as a single diastereomer.

evidence.text_span: α-Hydroxylation of the ester in compound 16 was performed by trapping the potassium enolate with oxygen and reducing the resulting hydroperoxide with triethylphosphite, giving compound 27 in 90% yield as a single diastereomer.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Acetate elimination can be achieved under basic or acidic conditions.
thought.gap: Achieving selective acetate elimination without decomposition or isomerization of the double bond.
thought.decision: To perform acetate elimination, chose screening of basic and acid conditions, expecting to obtain the desired alkene.

outcome.observation: Screening of basic and acid conditions for the acetate elimination led to either decomposition of the starting material or elimination and isomerization of the double bond in compound 29 to form the more stable endocyclic alkene 30.

evidence.text_span: Screening of basic and acid conditions for the acetate elimination led to either decomposition of the starting material or elimination and isomerization of the double bond in compound 29 to form the more stable endocyclic alkene 30.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Strong acids caused decomposition or isomerization during acetate elimination.
thought.gap: Finding milder conditions that promote elimination without side reactions.
thought.decision: To achieve acetate elimination, chose performing the reaction in acetone in the presence of I2 as a catalyst, expecting to obtain compound 29 with minimal isomerization.

outcome.observation: By performing the reaction in acetone in the presence of I2 as a catalyst, compound 29 could be obtained in a moderate yield, although as an inseparable 85:15 mixture with its exo-alkene isomer.

evidence.text_span: While relatively strong acids, such as MsOH or p-toluenesulfonic acid (PTSA), led to the clean formation of byproduct 30, we discovered that, by performing the reaction in acetone in the presence of I2 as a catalyst, compound 29 could be obtained in a moderate yield,21 although as an inseparable 85:15 mixture with its exo-alkene isomer.22
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Esters can be hydrolyzed to carboxylic acids via saponification.
thought.gap: Achieving complete saponification to yield aspterric acid.
thought.decision: To obtain aspterric acid, chose saponification of the ester with NaOH, expecting to obtain the target compound in good yield.

outcome.observation: Saponification of the ester afforded aspterric acid (2) in good yields as an 85:15 mixture with the exo-alkene isomer 31. NMR spectra matched authentic sample.

evidence.text_span: Finally, saponification of the ester afforded aspterric acid (2) in good yields as an 85:15 mixture with the exo-alkene isomer 31. The 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of synthesized compound 2 agreed with the spectroscopic data of a sample of the natural product.23
evidence.section: Synthesis of Common Intermediate 12 and Total Synthesis of (±)-Aspterric Acid

thought.background: Epoxidation and SN2' cyclization can be combined in a tandem reaction.
thought.gap: Achieving a reproducible and high-yielding tandem epoxidation/SN2' cyclization on compound 12.
thought.decision: To form the five-membered ring and alpha-hydroxylate the methyl ester, chose a one-pot tandem epoxidation/SN2' sequence on compound 12 using VO(acac)3 and TBHP, expecting to obtain product 33 in good yield.

outcome.observation: With the switch to directed epoxidation using VO(acac)3 and tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), the reaction delivered the desired product 33 in 71% yield.

evidence.text_span: For the synthesis of penigrisacid A, a one-pot tandem epoxidation 'SN2' sequence on compound 12 allowed the simultaneous formation of the five-membered ring and α-hydroxylation of the methyl ester (Scheme 3). Initial studies using mCPBA led to the formation of desired product 33 in moderate yields. However, the reaction was not easily reproducible, and in some cases, we detected epoxide intermediate 32 by 1H NMR of the crude reaction mixture,22 which decomposed upon attempted isolation. With the switch to directed epoxidation using VO(acac)3 (7.5 mol %) and tertbutyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), the reaction delivered the desired product 33 in 71% yield.
evidence.section: Synthesis of 3-epi-Penigrisacid A and Revised Penigrisacid A

thought.background: Hydrogenation can reduce double bonds, and acetate elimination can form new double bonds.
thought.gap: Achieving both hydrogenation and acetate elimination to form methyl ester 34.
thought.decision: To obtain methyl ester 34, chose hydrogenation of compound 33 with catalytic Pd(OH)2 and acid-mediated acetate elimination, expecting to confirm its structure by X-ray diffraction.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation of compound 33 with catalytic Pd(OH)2 in MeOH and acid-mediated acetate elimination afforded methyl ester 34 in 83% yield, whose structure was confirmed by X-ray diffraction.

evidence.text_span: Hydrogenation of compound 33 with catalytic Pd(OH)2 in MeOH and acid-mediated acetate elimination afforded methyl ester 34, whose structure was confirmed by X-ray diffraction (Scheme 3).
evidence.section: Synthesis of 3-epi-Penigrisacid A and Revised Penigrisacid A

thought.background: Esters can be hydrolyzed to carboxylic acids via saponification.
thought.gap: Achieving complete saponification to yield the target compound 4.
thought.decision: To obtain the target compound 4, chose saponification of the ester with NaOH, expecting to obtain the natural product.

outcome.observation: Saponification of the ester afforded target compound 4. However, 1H and 13C NMR spectra of synthetic compound 4 did not fully match the reported data, although NOESY experiments showed similar cross-peaks.

evidence.text_span: Finally, saponification of the ester with NaOH gave the target compound 4. However, 1H and 13C NMR spectra of synthetic compound 4 did not fully match the reported data,9 although the NOESY experiments showed similar cross-peaks, pointing to a closely similar structure.22 The structural assignment of the natural product was based on the assumption that the configuration at C-3 was R ,9 as displayed by other daucanes, including aspterric acid (2). This assumption was reasonable, because penigrisacid A was isolated from the same fermentation extract of Penicillium griseofulvum together with aspterric acid (2), penigrisacid C (5), and other metabolites, which have the R configuration at C-3.9 However, because C-3 is the only stereocenter that could not be assigned by NOESY correlations, we decided to synthesize the C-3 epimer of compound 4.
evidence.section: Synthesis of 3-epi-Penigrisacid A and Revised Penigrisacid A

thought.background: The NMR data for synthetic compound 4 did not match the reported data for penigrisacid A, suggesting a misassigned stereocenter at C-3 in the natural product.
thought.gap: Synthesizing the C-3 epimer of compound 4 to confirm the structural reassignment.
thought.decision: To synthesize the C-3 epimer, chose to convert compound 34 into xanthate 35, followed by deoxygenation under Barton−McCombie conditions, expecting to obtain compound 36.

outcome.observation: Compound 34 was converted into xanthate 35 in 79% yield.

evidence.text_span: However, because C-3 is the only stereocenter that could not be assigned by NOESY correlations, we decided to synthesize the C-3 epimer of compound 4. Thus, compound 34 was converted into xanthate 35, which was deoxygenated under Barton−McCombie conditions to give compound 36 in 60% yield over two steps.
evidence.section: Synthesis of 3-epi-Penigrisacid A and Revised Penigrisacid A

thought.background: Xanthates can be deoxygenated under Barton−McCombie conditions.
thought.gap: Achieving efficient deoxygenation to obtain compound 36.
thought.decision: To deoxygenate xanthate 35, chose Barton−McCombie conditions using AIBN and nBu3SnH, expecting to obtain compound 36.

outcome.observation: Deoxygenation under Barton−McCombie conditions gave compound 36 in 77% yield.

evidence.text_span: Thus, compound 34 was converted into xanthate 35, which was deoxygenated under Barton−McCombie conditions to give compound 36 in 60% yield over two steps.
evidence.section: Synthesis of 3-epi-Penigrisacid A and Revised Penigrisacid A

thought.background: Alpha-hydroxylation of esters can be achieved via enolate formation and reaction with oxygen, followed by reduction.
thought.gap: Achieving selective alpha-hydroxylation of the ester in 36 to obtain 37.
thought.decision: To alpha-hydroxylate the ester, chose oxidation of the enolate of compound 36 under conditions similar to those employed in the synthesis of aspterric acid (2), expecting to obtain compound 37.

outcome.observation: Oxidation of the enolate of compound 36 gave compound 37. Yield for this step is not explicitly stated but is part of a 2-step sequence yielding 60% for 36.

evidence.text_span: Oxidation of the enolate of compound 36 under conditions similar to those employed in the synthesis of aspterric acid (2) gave compound 37
evidence.section: Synthesis of 3-epi-Penigrisacid A and Revised Penigrisacid A

thought.background: Esters can be hydrolyzed to carboxylic acids via saponification.
thought.gap: Achieving complete saponification to yield penigrisacid A (4a).
thought.decision: To obtain penigrisacid A (4a), chose saponification of compound 37 with NaOH, expecting its NMR spectra to match the natural product.

outcome.observation: Saponification provided penigrisacid A (4a) in 89% yield, whose NMR spectra matched with those reported for the natural product.

evidence.text_span: which upon saponification provided penigrisacid A (4a), whose NMR spectra matched with those reported for the natural product.9
evidence.section: Synthesis of 3-epi-Penigrisacid A and Revised Penigrisacid A

thought.background: Gold(I)-catalyzed cyclization/cycloaddition can form the hydroazulene core.
thought.gap: Achieving the cyclization/cycloaddition and subsequent deprotection to yield ketone 38.
thought.decision: To form the hydroazulene core and obtain ketone 38, chose gold(I)-catalyzed cyclization/cycloaddition followed by treatment with Et3N·3HF, expecting to shorten the previous synthetic route to schisanwilsonene A.

outcome.observation: After the gold(I)-catalyzed cyclization/cycloaddition, treatment of the silyl enol ether intermediate with Et3N·3HF in THF delivered ketone 38 in 49% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: After the gold(I)-catalyzed cyclization/cycloaddition, treatment of the silyl enol ether intermediate with Et3N·3HF in THF delivered ketone 38 in 49% yield over two steps.
evidence.section: Formal Synthesis of (±)-Schisanwilsonene A

thought.background: Ketones can be converted to vinyl triflates and then undergo palladium-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation.
thought.gap: Achieving efficient triflation and carbonylation to introduce the ester group.
thought.decision: To introduce the ester group, chose a two-step sequence of triflation/Pd-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation, expecting to obtain methyl ester 39.

outcome.observation: The two-step sequence of triflation/Pd-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation gave methyl ester 39 in 79% yield.

evidence.text_span: The two-step sequence of triflation/Pd-catalyzed methoxycarbonilation gave methyl ester 39.
evidence.section: Formal Synthesis of (±)-Schisanwilsonene A

thought.background: Acetate groups can be removed via methanolysis.
thought.gap: Achieving efficient methanolysis to yield compound 40.
thought.decision: To remove the acetate group, chose methanolysis of methyl ester 39 with NaOMe, expecting to obtain compound 40.

outcome.observation: Methanolysis of the acetate group gave compound 40 in 73% yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, methanolysis of the acetate group gave compound 40 in 73% yield
evidence.section: Formal Synthesis of (±)-Schisanwilsonene A

thought.background: Double bonds can be reduced via hydrogenation.
thought.gap: Achieving efficient hydrogenation to yield compound 41.
thought.decision: To reduce the double bond, chose hydrogenation of compound 40 with Pd/C, expecting to afford compound 41.

outcome.observation: Compound 40 was hydrogenated to afford compound 41 in 80% yield as a 6:1 diastereomeric ratio (dr) mixture.

evidence.text_span: which was hydrogenated to afford compound 41 in 80% yield. Product 41, a late intermediate of our previous total synthesis,7 was obtained as a 6:1 diastereomeric ratio (dr) mixture. Thus, the formal synthesis of schisanwilsonene A (3) was achieved in 11 steps (longest linear sequence) and an approximate overall 10% yield.
evidence.section: Formal Synthesis of (±)-Schisanwilsonene A

validation.significance: This work provides a divergent total synthesis of three daucane natural products, including the first total synthesis and structural reassignment of penigrisacid A. The gold(I)-catalyzed cycloisomerization/formal (4+3) cycloaddition is highlighted as an efficient key step. The synthesis of (±)-aspterric acid was achieved in 15 steps and ca. 2% overall yield, and a formal synthesis of (±)-schisanwilsonene A was achieved in 11 steps and ca. 10% overall yield. The enantioselective synthesis of 3-epi-penigrisacid A was also completed.

source.title: Synthesis of Daucane Natural Products Enabled by a Gold(I)-Catalyzed Tandem Cycloisomerization/(4 + 3) Cycloaddition.
source.authors: Martí À, Armengol-Relats H, Sadurní A, Pérez-Puigdomènech MÀ, Echavarren AM.
